ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general instant coffee beverage class; no specific 3-in-1 less-sugar premix subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers instant coffee powder; the 3-in-1 premix with creamer and reduced sugar is a more specific formulation not separately coded. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34111008 covers coffee as a substance; no specific 3-in-1 premix concept exists. Closest defensible match used.

allergens: may contain milk derivatives (non-dairy creamer may contain casein)

serving.description: 1 sachet prepared as directed (approximately 200 ml beverage)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 15–20 g powder) dissolved in 180–200 ml of hot water, yielding roughly 200 g of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Coffee (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '3 in 1 coffee (less sugar)', cr_id D09010007. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous instant coffee mix entry (NDB 14209) as approximately 1.2 g/100g of total fat 1.55 g, consistent with palm-based non-dairy creamer composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 3-in-1 instant coffee (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 53 based on the analogous entry for sweetened instant coffee mix with non-dairy creamer from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports GI ~53 for similar sugar-containing instant coffee premixes. A GI of 53 falls within the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification standard. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (53 × 8.64 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2) / 100 = 9.2 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content 5.55 g/100 g in a liquid vehicle; sodium is low at 26 mg/100 g but sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this beverage because its sugar content (5.55 g/100 g) exceeds the AHA 2018 threshold for sugar-sweetened beverages that independently raise blood pressure; switching to unsweetened black coffee or a no-added-sugar variant is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.55 g sugar/100 g and low dietary fibre (0.35 g/100 g); liquid sugars raise postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage because it is a sugar-sweetened liquid with 5.55 g sugar per 100 g and only 0.35 g fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the ADA recommends choosing truly unsweetened coffee or water instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from palm-based non-dairy creamer (~1.2 g/100 g) combined with added sugar (5.55 g/100 g) contributes to atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this beverage due to its saturated fat content from palm-based non-dairy creamer and added sugar load, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles; plain black coffee without creamer or sugar is the preferred alternative per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.55 g/100 g) and likely palm-based saturated fat in creamer promote pro-inflammatory pathways; minimal fibre (0.35 g/100 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage because its sugar and saturated fat content promote systemic inflammation; switching to unsweetened black coffee preserves coffee polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar and creamer load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Instant Coffee (Less Sugar) is an ultra-processed instant beverage premix widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It combines instant coffee granules, non-dairy creamer (typically palm-oil-based), and a reduced quantity of sugar in a single sachet, designed to be dissolved in hot water for immediate consumption. The 'less sugar' designation indicates a lower sugar content compared to the standard 3-in-1 formulation, though at 5.55 g sugar per 100 g of prepared beverage it still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage under AHA and ADA definitions. The beverage is light brown, mildly sweet, and creamy with a mild coffee flavour. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 51 kcal, 0.59 g protein, 1.55 g fat (predominantly saturated from the palm-based creamer), and 8.64 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is low at 0.35 g/100 g and sodium is low at 26 mg/100 g. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its sugar content and the rapid glycaemic response associated with liquid sugar. The saturated fat from the non-dairy creamer also raises cardiovascular concern. It is commonly consumed at breakfast and is a staple of the Singaporean and Malaysian office and home morning routine. Halal-certified variants are widely available. The product is not suitable for vegans due to potential casein content in the non-dairy creamer.
common_user_queries: Is 3-in-1 coffee less sugar good for diabetics?
Can I drink 3-in-1 coffee if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a sachet of 3-in-1 less sugar coffee?
What is the sugar content of 3-in-1 instant coffee less sugar?
Is premixed instant coffee suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010007, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 14209 (Beverages, coffee, instant, with whitener and sugar) as the HPB source does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g based on typical palm-based non-dairy creamer formulation with no hydrogenated fat declared. 2. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 g as non-dairy creamer is plant-derived and instant coffee contains no cholesterol. 3. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry and are not available in a directly analogous USDA entry for this specific formulation. 4. GI value of 53 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 53 is classified as LOW per the University of Sydney standard (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in this iteration. 5. Halal status is typical for commercially available 3-in-1 coffee in Singapore but should be verified against the specific brand's certification. 6. The 'less sugar' label does not render this product suitable for blood glucose management; it remains a sugar-sweetened beverage by clinical definition.
